name: Alternate Electors
description: The concept of 'Alternate Electors' in the American context refers to the belief that a slate of electors different from the one certified by a state's official election results could or should be seated in the Electoral College. This theory often arises in the wake of close or contested presidential elections, fueled by claims of widespread voter fraud or irregularities. Proponents argue that these 'alternate electors,' selected by state legislatures or other means, represent the 'true' will of the people and should be recognized by Congress. The origin of this idea can be traced back to historical disputes over election results and interpretations of the Constitution's provisions regarding the Electoral College. Evidence cited by proponents typically includes anecdotal accounts of voting anomalies, statistical analyses purporting to show irregularities, and legal arguments challenging the validity of election procedures. Mainstream scholars and legal experts overwhelmingly reject the notion of alternate electors as a legitimate mechanism for overturning election results. They point to the established legal framework for certifying elections, resolving disputes through recounts and judicial review, and the limited role of state legislatures in selecting electors after a popular vote has been held. The idea is seen as a dangerous attempt to subvert democratic processes and undermine the rule of law, lacking constitutional basis and potentially leading to political instability. The legal precedent also strongly opposes the possibility, with courts consistently upholding the certified election results. Attempts to seat alternate electors have been largely unsuccessful and often face legal challenges and public condemnation.
subtitle: Challenging the Electoral College certification.

claims.claim: Alternate electors represent the 'true' will of the people when elections are fraudulent.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal reports of voter fraud, statistical anomalies in vote counts, and claims of manipulated voting machines.
claims.criticism: These claims are often unsubstantiated or debunked by fact-checkers, audits, and court rulings. Statistical anomalies can often be explained by normal variations in voting patterns or demographic shifts.

claims.claim: State legislatures have the power to appoint alternate electors if they believe an election was compromised.
claims.evidence: Interpretations of Article II, Section 1 of the Constitution, which grants states power to appoint electors.
claims.criticism: This interpretation is disputed by legal scholars who argue that this power is limited by the 12th Amendment and subsequent legal precedents that establish the primacy of the popular vote in determining electors. The National Conference of State Legislatures also disagrees with this interpretation.

claims.claim: Congress has the authority to choose between competing slates of electors.
claims.evidence: The Electoral Count Act of 1887, which outlines procedures for Congress to resolve disputes over electoral votes.
claims.criticism: The Electoral Count Act is subject to interpretation, but most legal scholars agree it is intended to provide a mechanism for resolving legitimate disputes, not for overturning validly certified election results based on unsubstantiated claims of fraud. Furthermore, the act was amended in 2022 to clarify that state's certifications are presumed valid.

keyFigures.name: Rudy Giuliani
keyFigures.role: Prominent advocate for alternate electors following the 2020 election.

keyFigures.name: Marc E. Elias
keyFigures.role: Attorney who led legal challenges against attempts to seat alternate electors.

mythologyConnections: Trickster archetype - attempts to subvert established order
The 'true king' narrative - belief in a rightful leader denied power

timeline.event: Disputed presidential election between Hayes and Tilden leads to a compromise in which a special electoral commission awards the election to Hayes.
Bush v. Gore Supreme Court case resolves a contested election, highlighting the importance of certified election results.
Widespread claims of voter fraud following the presidential election lead to attempts to seat alternate electors in several states.

sources.author: Elie Mystal
sources.work: Allow Me to Retort: A Black Guy's Guide to the Constitution
sources.title: Elie Mystal

worshipPractices.name: Ritual of Recounting
worshipPractices.description: This practice involves meticulous examination of records and pronouncements, seeking hidden meanings or inconsistencies. Followers believe that uncovering these discrepancies can reveal the 'true' will of the populace, even if it differs from official accounts. Recitations of election law are central to the ritual.

worshipPractices.name: Invocation of Precedent
worshipPractices.description: Adherents invoke historical instances of disputed elections or electoral challenges, drawing parallels to the present situation. These invocations are meant to legitimize their cause and call upon the 'spirits' of past struggles for guidance. Legal texts and historical documents serve as sacred objects.

festivals.name: The Day of Certification
festivals.description: This day marks the official certification of election results, and is met with either celebration or protest depending on the follower's allegiance. Vigils are held, petitions are circulated, and pronouncements are made challenging or supporting the outcome. It is a day of heightened tension and activity.

festivals.name: The Assembly of the Electoral College
festivals.description: During this time, followers engage in symbolic acts of defiance or support, depending on their stance. They may organize mock electoral votes, hold rallies, or disseminate information aimed at influencing the electors' decisions. It is a period of intense lobbying and public pressure.

cultural.modernLegacy: The concept of 'Alternate Electors' continues to manifest in contemporary political discourse and legal challenges, particularly following closely contested elections. It fuels debates about election integrity, voter suppression, and the legitimacy of democratic processes, often influencing political activism and policy reform. The idea has also entered popular fiction, serving as a plot device for exploring hypothetical political scenarios and power struggles.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: The Einherjar are chosen warriors destined to fight in Ragnarok, representing an alternate, divinely-selected force to replace the current order, similar to the idea of alternate electors seeking to replace the certified ones.

The Weighing of the Heart ceremony determines whether a soul is worthy of entering the afterlife, acting as a final, alternative judgment to an individual's earthly life, mirroring the potential role of alternate electors as a different final decision point.

Themis represents divine law and order, but can also be invoked to challenge or correct perceived injustices within the existing legal framework, acting as an alternate source of authority when human law is seen as flawed, akin to the rationale behind alternate electors.

Vishnu descends to Earth in various avatars to restore Dharma (cosmic order) when it is threatened, representing an alternate manifestation of divine power intervening to correct a perceived imbalance, much like alternate electors are proposed to correct an election's outcome.

associations.name: Chaos
associations.significance: Alternate electors represent a disruption of established order and a descent into political instability, mirroring the primordial chaos found in many mythologies.

associations.name: Serpent
associations.significance: The serpent, often associated with deception and hidden agendas, reflects the potential for manipulation and subversion inherent in the concept of alternate electors.

associations.name: Ambrosia
associations.significance: While not inherently negative, the pursuit of alternate electors can be seen as chasing a 'divine' but ultimately unattainable and potentially harmful outcome, like a misplaced desire for Ambrosia.

associations.name: Quicksilver
associations.significance: Quicksilver's fluid and unstable nature represents the unpredictable and potentially volatile consequences of attempting to seat alternate electors.

associations.name: Two
associations.significance: The concept hinges on a division, creating two opposing factions vying for legitimacy, echoing the duality found in many creation myths.

associations.name: Eclipse
associations.significance: An eclipse, a temporary obscuring of the true light, symbolizes the temporary suspension of established electoral processes by the introduction of alternate electors.

associations.name: Pandora's Box
associations.significance: Opening the door to alternate electors could unleash unforeseen and potentially devastating consequences on the democratic process, similar to the unleashing of evils from Pandora's Box.

companions.name: Hermes
companions.description: As the messenger of the gods, Hermes could swiftly deliver messages and influence perceptions, potentially swaying electors or uncovering hidden information. His cunning and persuasive abilities would be invaluable in navigating complex political landscapes.

companions.name: Argus Panoptes
companions.description: With his hundred eyes, Argus would provide unparalleled surveillance, ensuring the electors' safety and guarding against any attempts at coercion or manipulation. His vigilance would deter foul play and maintain the integrity of the electoral process.

companions.name: Themis
companions.description: Themis, the Greek goddess of divine law and order, would lend her unwavering commitment to justice and fairness, ensuring the electors adhere to proper legal procedures. Her presence guarantees an objective and unbiased outcome.

companions.name: Ma'at
companions.description: The Egyptian goddess of truth, justice, and cosmic balance. Her feather could weigh the hearts and intentions of the electors, revealing any dishonesty or corruption.

extendedContent.title: The Shadow King: Divine Understudies and Substitutes
extendedContent.content:
Across various mythologies, the concept of a 'shadow king' or divine substitute appears, often to avert prophecies or appease capricious deities. These figures, acting as alternate rulers or sacrificial lambs, represent a form of 'alternate elector,' shouldering the burdens and consequences meant for the intended leader. In ancient Mesopotamia, the king would sometimes abdicate for a brief period, a commoner taking his place to absorb any impending divine wrath foretold by astrologers. This 'substitute king' was treated with royal honors, only to be sacrificed at the end of his reign, effectively cleansing the land and restoring the true king. 

Similar themes appear in ancient Egyptian mythology. While not strictly 'alternate electors,' pharaohs often commissioned statues and images of themselves to act as repositories for their ka (soul). These statues served as symbolic stand-ins, capable of receiving offerings and maintaining the pharaoh's presence even in death. The story of Osiris and Set also involves a form of substitution, where Horus avenges his father, indirectly acting as a 'voter' for Osiris's rightful claim to the throne of the underworld. These stories highlight the deep-seated human need to have backup plans and to navigate fate through indirect means. 

The use of scapegoats in ancient Greek rituals, like the Pharmakos, reflects a collective desire to transfer societal ills onto a designated individual, effectively 'electing' them to bear the community's burdens. While not a direct parallel to electoral politics, this practice demonstrates the tendency to designate individuals to embody and resolve societal problems, mirroring the perceived role of electors in ensuring a stable transfer of power.

extendedContent.title: The Divine Proxy: Champions and Chosen Vessels
extendedContent.content:
Many mythologies feature heroes or champions chosen by deities to act as their representatives on Earth. These figures, often imbued with divine powers or guidance, can be seen as 'alternate electors' in the sense that they embody the will of a higher power and exert influence on mortal affairs. In Norse mythology, champions like Sigurd were often favored by Odin, receiving his blessing and acting as instruments of his will. Sigurd's actions, driven by Odin's influence, shaped the course of events and ultimately contributed to Ragnarok, the twilight of the gods. 

Similarly, in the Mahabharata, Arjuna is guided by Krishna, who acts as his charioteer and divine advisor. Krishna's counsel shapes Arjuna's decisions, particularly during the Kurukshetra War, effectively making Krishna an 'alternate elector' guiding Arjuna’s actions on the battlefield. Krishna's role transcends mere advice; he embodies the divine dharma and uses Arjuna as a vessel to uphold cosmic order. The stories of Joan of Arc and other divinely inspired figures throughout history also echo this archetype, where individuals claim to act on behalf of a higher power, influencing political and social landscapes. 

These examples highlight the persistent belief in divinely appointed intermediaries who shape the course of history, representing a form of 'alternate electorate' that operates beyond the realm of human politics. The actions of these chosen vessels are often perceived as divinely ordained, lending them an authority that transcends earthly power structures.

extendedContent.title: The Oracle's Verdict: Prophecy and Indirect Election
extendedContent.content:
Oracles, seers, and prophets across various mythologies often played a role akin to 'indirect electors,' their prophecies influencing the decisions of rulers and shaping the course of history. In ancient Greece, the Oracle of Delphi was consulted by individuals and city-states seeking guidance on important matters. The Oracle's pronouncements, often cryptic and open to interpretation, held immense sway, influencing political alliances, military campaigns, and even the selection of leaders. While not directly 'electing' individuals, the Oracle's pronouncements could sway public opinion and legitimize certain candidates or policies. 

The Sibylline Books in Roman mythology served a similar purpose, containing prophecies that were consulted in times of crisis. These books held the potential to influence senatorial decisions and shape the course of Roman history. The interpretation of these prophecies, often subject to political maneuvering, could effectively 'elect' certain courses of action or legitimize specific leaders. The prophetic pronouncements served as a powerful force in Roman society, shaping political decisions and influencing the selection of leaders, indirectly acting as powerful electors.

These examples demonstrate how prophecy, even when ambiguous, could exert a significant influence on political outcomes. The interpretation of prophecies became a form of indirect election, shaping public opinion and legitimizing certain candidates or policies. The authority of the oracle or prophet, often perceived as a conduit to divine knowledge, made their pronouncements a powerful force in shaping the course of history.

extendedContent.title: The Council of Elders: Collective Guardians of Tradition
extendedContent.content:
Many cultures feature councils of elders or wise figures who serve as guardians of tradition and wield considerable influence in decision-making processes. These councils, representing a collective form of 'alternate electors,' often play a crucial role in selecting leaders, mediating disputes, and upholding societal norms. In many indigenous cultures, elders are revered for their wisdom and experience, and their decisions carry significant weight within the community. They often participate in ceremonies and rituals that legitimize leadership transitions and ensure the continuity of cultural practices. 

The concept of the Areopagus in ancient Athens, initially a council of former archons, reflects a similar structure. Though its power diminished over time, it initially held considerable influence in matters of law and justice, potentially influencing the selection of leaders and shaping Athenian policy. The Sanhedrin in ancient Judaism served as a council of elders responsible for interpreting Jewish law and administering justice. While not directly involved in selecting rulers, the Sanhedrin's pronouncements on religious and legal matters could significantly influence the political landscape. 

These examples highlight the importance of collective wisdom and experience in shaping societal decisions. Councils of elders, acting as 'alternate electors,' often serve as a check on power, ensuring that decisions are made in accordance with established traditions and cultural values. Their influence extends beyond formal political structures, shaping public opinion and legitimizing leadership transitions.

extendedContent.title: Trickster Gods and Unforeseen Outcomes: Chaos as an Elector
extendedContent.content:
Trickster gods, like Loki in Norse mythology or Hermes in Greek mythology, represent a form of 'alternate elector' driven by chaos and unpredictability. Their actions, often motivated by mischief or self-interest, can disrupt established power structures and lead to unforeseen outcomes. Loki's actions, for example, frequently destabilized the realm of the gods, contributing to the events leading up to Ragnarok. While not directly 'electing' leaders, Loki's disruptive influence created opportunities for new power dynamics to emerge. 

Similarly, Hermes, the messenger of the gods, was known for his cunning and ability to deceive. His interventions in mortal affairs often led to unexpected consequences, disrupting established plans and altering the course of events. The trickster archetype represents a force of change that operates outside the realm of established authority. Their actions, while often destructive, can also pave the way for innovation and new possibilities. The actions of such trickster figures serve as a reminder that unforeseen events and unpredictable actors can significantly influence political outcomes, acting as an 'alternate electorate' that challenges established norms.

The role of chaos and chance in shaping historical events is often underestimated. The actions of individuals motivated by self-interest, ambition, or even mere accident can have profound consequences, disrupting established power structures and creating opportunities for new leaders to emerge. The trickster archetype serves as a reminder that the course of history is not always determined by rational planning or deliberate action, but can also be shaped by the forces of chaos and unpredictability.
